history.title: Conversation history
insight.welcome: Hi! 👋 I'm your Buyer Dashboard cost analyst. I've loaded **5 years of data** (Jan 2021 – Dec 2025). Ask me anything — or watch the automatic insights appear.
insight.dominantComponent: 📊 In your model, **{{component}}** accounts for **{{pct}}% of should cost** — it's your main cost driver and your biggest negotiation lever.
insight.shouldCostUp: 📈 Should cost increased by **{{pct}}%** vs Jan 2021 baseline — that's **+€{{abs}}** per unit. Is your contract price keeping up with the market?
insight.shouldCostDown: 📉 Good news — should cost dropped by **{{pct}}%** vs Jan 2021, saving **€{{abs}}** per unit. That's a strong argument for renegotiation.
insight.biggestDriver: ⚠️ **{{component}}** is the biggest cost driver — responsible for **+€{{abs}}** ({{pct}}% of should cost) since baseline.
insight.biggestSaver: 💡 **{{component}}** is cheaper today than in 2021 — saving you **€{{abs}}** per unit ({{pct}}% of should cost). Check if your contract reflects this.
insight.weightedSpike: 📌 **{{component}}** ({{weight}}% of cost) spiked **{{pct}}%** in {{date}}. At this model weight — that's a meaningful margin impact.
insight.marginHigh: 🔴 According to your model, the supplier may be charging **{{pct}}% above should cost**. That's potential negotiation room.
insight.marginLow: 🟢 Supplier price is **{{pct}}% below should cost** — you're in a good position.
tooltip.shouldCost: Should Cost is the theoretically fair price — the sum of weighted cost components in EUR. Each component = (market price / baseline) × weight% × base price.
tooltip.costModel: The model defines your product's cost structure. Each material has a weight — they must sum to 100%. Should Cost is the sum of these weighted EUR components.
tooltip.basePrice: Reference price for comparison with Should Cost. Margin = Reference Price - Should Cost.
tooltip.supplierPrice: The price your supplier charges — entered as a time series (date + price) so you can compare it against should cost on the chart.
tooltip.csvImport: Import your supplier price history from a CSV file. Format: two columns — date (yyyy-MM) and price (EUR). Download the template to get started.
tooltip.index: The index shows how prices changed vs Jan 2021 = 100. Index 120 means prices are 20% higher than Jan 2021.
tooltip.steel: Global hot-rolled steel coil prices ($/t). Key driver for metal components, machinery and structures.
tooltip.aluminum: Global aluminum prices ($/t). Critical for lightweight components, packaging and automotive.
tooltip.transport: Freight and logistics cost index. Covers road, sea and air transport.
tooltip.energy: Energy price index (electricity + gas). Affects production costs across all industries.
macro.noCategory: Create a category first to analyze cost impact.
macro.aiPrompt: Explain the impact of "{{event}}" ({{value}}) on my cost model. Affects: {{affects}}. Insight: {{insight}}
